main_summary.summary: Esther M. A. Terry, interviewed July 6, 2011, on the campus of Bennett College by Joe Mosnier for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her path from a sharecropping family in Wise, Warren County, North Carolina, to the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960 and on to the founding of W. E. B. Du Bois African American Studies at UMass Amherst. The youngest of twelve, Terry describes her mother Mary Alexander's commitment to schooling and her father's purchase of the family farm in 1945. She entered Bennett College on a scholarship after the 1957 extension of Social Security to farmers gave her parents a check. At Bennett she studied English under Hobart Jarrett, grammar under Dr. Crawford, theater under Fred Eady, and lived in the Honors Dorm under the leadership of Dr. Willa B. Player. Terry participated in the Greensboro sit-ins at the F.W. Woolworth lunch counter beginning February 1, 1960, alongside Bennett College student activists Roslyn Smith, Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, Dolores Finger, Gloria Brown, and Linda Brown. After graduating in spring 1961 she earned a master's at UNC-Chapel Hill and a doctorate at UMass Amherst, where she helped build the African American Studies program with Michael Thelwell, Bernard W. Bell, Eugene Terry, and Sidney Kaplan. She returned to Bennett as Provost in 2009.
key_themes: Greensboro sit-ins and Bennett College student activists
Black women's education at a historically Black private college
Dr. Willa B. Player's institutional leadership and protection of student protest
Founding of African American Studies at UMass Amherst
Family farm, Social Security extension to farmers, and access to higher education
main_summary.historical_significance:
Terry's testimony documents the planning role of Bennett College women in the Greensboro sit-ins, a counter-narrative to the standard account that centers only the four North Carolina A&T State University men who sat down on February 1, 1960. She describes a deliberated plan that originated among Bennett sociology students, was reviewed by Dr. Hobart Jarrett and Dr. Willa B. Player, and was timed by Player to follow Christmas vacation so that participants would not scatter mid-campaign. The interview also records Player's drive to the Greensboro jail to claim arrested Bennett students and the cross-burning at the president's house.

Terry's account of UMass Amherst documents the founding pipeline of one of the first PhD-granting African American Studies departments in the country: the Sterling A. Brown-to-Sidney Kaplan referral chain out of Howard University, the prior 1959 dedication of The Massachusetts Review to the A&T Four, and the multiracial faculty-student coalition that secured department status without the protest violence that marked similar efforts at peer institutions.

chapters.title: Family and Farm in Wise, North Carolina
chapters.summary: Terry describes growing up the youngest of twelve children on a family farm in Wise, Warren County, North Carolina. Her mother Mary Alexander, widowed by the 1918 flu epidemic from first husband Arthur Hunt, returned to Warren County with three children and married Terry's father, who raised all the children together. Her father moved from sharecropping to ownership, buying the land in 1945.

chapters.title: Church, School, and a Mother's Ethic
chapters.summary: Terry recounts her mother's churchgoing without fanaticism, her preference for teaching over preaching, and the family rule against speaking of white people as an undifferentiated 'they.' She describes farm labor schedules that pulled children from school and her mother's compensating labor in the cotton field.
related_events: segregated schooling in 1940s rural North Carolina

chapters.title: Social Security and the Path to College
chapters.summary: Terry explains how the 1957 extension of Social Security to farmers, with retroactive eligibility to age 65, gave her parents a check that funded her college tuition. She describes helping neighbors document their ages through family Bibles and midwife memory when birth certificates were missing.
related_events: 1957 Social Security extension to farm workers

chapters.title: Arrival at Bennett College
chapters.summary: Terry describes her family's arrival on the Bennett College campus, the bond formed that day between her mother and Roslyn Smith's mother, and the mutual mothering arrangement that made Smith her lifelong closest friend. She names Theaoseus Tobias Clayton Sr as a Warren County connection.

chapters.title: Bennett Faculty and the English Department
chapters.summary: Terry describes her Bennett English faculty: Dr. Hobart Jarrett's exacting literary training and intellectual showmanship, Dr. Crawford's grammar drills and sentence diagramming, and theater director Fred Eady, whose marriage Terry and her classmates engineered. She lived in the Honors Dorm.

chapters.title: Bennett's Ethic and the Segregated City
chapters.summary: Terry contrasts the protected life inside Bennett's gates, where students dressed for dinner and learned which fork to use, with the segregated downtown Greensboro of white-only signs and the F.W. Woolworth lunch counter. She describes the cognitive dissonance that made student action inevitable.

chapters.title: Planning the Greensboro Sit-ins
chapters.summary: Terry recounts the planning of the Greensboro sit-ins among Bennett sociology students Roslyn Smith, Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, Gloria Brown, and others. She describes Dr. Hobart Jarrett's advice to include North Carolina A&T State University men, and Dr. Willa B. Player's counsel to wait until after Christmas vacation so participants would not scatter.
related_events: fall 1959 sit-in planning at Bennett College

chapters.title: February 1, 1960 and the Sit-ins
chapters.summary: Terry describes Gwen Mackel's account of standing inside Woolworth's on February 1, 1960, having bought something so as not to be ejected, waiting as part of a planned action when the A&T four sat down. Terry herself sat at the lunch counter alongside Linda Brown and other Bennett College student activists, encountering the hatred of onlookers.

chapters.title: Dr. Willa B. Player's Leadership
chapters.summary: Terry describes Bennett College president Dr. Willa B. Player as a tall, quiet, dignified woman with a profound commitment to social justice. Player allowed students to march and sit-in but required them to keep their grades, drove alone to the Greensboro jail to claim arrested Bennett students during a second wave of arrests, and had a cross burned at the president's house.
related_events: second wave of Greensboro sit-in arrests (Feb-April 1960)

chapters.title: UNC-Chapel Hill Master's Degree
chapters.summary: Terry describes her father's insistence that as a taxpayer his daughter could attend UNC-Chapel Hill rather than be paid by the state to go north. She recounts the housing office's surprise on arrival that she was Black, having paired her with a white roommate, and her mother's reminder that 'it's not the girls, it's the ruling.' Terry studied with C. Hugh Holman and earned the master's.
related_events: UNC-Chapel Hill graduate school integration period (early 1960s)

chapters.title: Founding African American Studies at UMass Amherst
chapters.summary: Terry describes arriving at UMass Amherst with Michael Thelwell, Bernard W. Bell, and her future husband Eugene Terry. Sterling A. Brown at Howard University had been redirecting graduate students to Sidney Kaplan at UMass Amherst, who had helped found The Massachusetts Review. The 1959 first issue had been dedicated to the A&T Four. Terry helped build the African American Studies department, supported by a multiracial coalition.
related_events: UMass Amherst W. E. B. Du Bois Department founding (late 1960s-early 1970s)
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party at 1964 Democratic Convention in Atlantic City

chapters.title: Return as Provost and a Charge to Students
chapters.summary: Terry reflects on her 2009 return to Bennett College as Provost. She tells current students that the segregation signs are down but that economic injustice remains, and that each generation runs only its own lap in the relay. She charges Bennett women with carrying social justice forward alongside whatever discipline they pursue.
